speaker: ALEX
text: Welcome to episode 31 of Git Going with GitHub: GitHub Codespaces. I am Alex, and today we are teaching this topic as a conversation you can carry into the workshop, not as a page you have to memorize.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And I am Jamie. I will keep pulling the lesson back to real learners, real repositories, and the evidence that proves the work happened.

speaker: ALEX
text: The lesson focus is Cloud dev environments, accessibility setup, and screen reader usage. We will treat every step as a teachable decision, because that is what makes the skill portable.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So we should explain the why clearly enough that the steps make sense when the learner meets them later.

speaker: ALEX
text: That is it. If a listener only has audio right now, they should still get the complete concept and know what evidence would prove success.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Okay, set the room for us. What are we walking into?

speaker: ALEX
text: Cloud Development Environments - Accessibility Guide: GitHub Codespaces gives you a full VS Code development environment in your browser or connected to your local VS Code, running on a cloud machine. The next useful detail is concrete: For screen reader users, this means a consistent, pre-configured environment with no local setup required.

speaker: ALEX
text: The next layer is this. Here is the learner-facing version. A Codespace is a cloud-hosted development environment. Put another way, when you open one, GitHub provisions a virtual machine, clones your repository into it, and connects it to a VS Code interface - either in your browser or via your local VS Code Desktop. A good GitHub workflow is like a well-run meeting: everyone knows the topic, the next action, and who has the floor.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What is the one idea that makes the next few steps less mysterious?

speaker: ALEX
text: This is the move inside Why this matters for accessibility: free tier: GitHub provides a monthly free allowance of Codespace hours for personal accounts. That matters in practice: Workshop usage typically falls well within the free tier.

speaker: ALEX
text: That shows up in the workshop in a few specific ways. No local installation of tools, compilers, or language runtimes required. Your screen reader and OS settings stay on your machine; only the editor runs in the cloud. VS Code's full accessibility features (audio cues, screen reader optimized mode, diff viewer) are available. The environment is identical for every workshop participant - facilitators can reproduce issues reliably.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That feels much more doable when you say it as one move.

speaker: ALEX
text: Right. The magic is not speed. The magic is knowing what changed and why it matters.

speaker: ALEX
text: Now bring the learner back to the room. Learning Cards: Cloud Editors Overview has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: For a learner, the useful signals are concrete. Codespaces gives you a full VS Code interface with terminal access -- your screen reader and OS settings stay on your local machine. The browser-based editor activates screen reader optimized mode automatically if your OS signals a screen reader is running. Connect a Codespace to your local VS Code Desktop (via the Codespaces extension) for the most familiar screen reader experience. Settings Sync carries your font size, theme, and zoom preferences from local VS Code into Codespaces automatically. The browser-based editor supports Ctrl+= / Ctrl+- zoom just like desktop VS Code. Use a high-contrast VS Code theme -- it applies identically in the cloud editor.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What is the ordered workflow?

speaker: ALEX
text: From a Repository Page has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: The path is straightforward once it is named. Step one is navigate to the repository on GitHub. Step two is press the Code button (keyboard shortcut: there is no direct shortcut - Tab to the button). Step three is in the panel that opens, choose the Codespaces tab. Step four is activate Create codespace on main (or your branch name). That is the rhythm: orient, act, verify, continue.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Give me the sequence, because order matters here.

speaker: ALEX
text: First, the Codespace opens in a new browser tab after provisioning (typically 30-60 seconds). That small check between steps is what makes the workflow reliable.

speaker: ALEX
text: The useful habit is simple: orient, act, verify, then continue. That pause between action and trust is part of the work.

speaker: JAMIE
text: How would you walk the room through that step by step?

speaker: ALEX
text: From the Codespaces Dashboard has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: First, go to github.com/codespaces. Then, activate New codespace. After that, use the search field to find your repository. Finally, choose a branch, machine type, and region. That small check between steps is what makes the workflow reliable.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What does the learner do first, second, and then after that?

speaker: ALEX
text: Start here: Activate Create codespace. The sequence works because every action has a confirmation.

speaker: JAMIE
text: If I am listening before the workshop starts, what should settle in my mind first?

speaker: ALEX
text: If the interface shifts, Machine Types is still useful because for workshop exercises, the default 2-core machine is sufficient.

speaker: ALEX
text: This is where the talk moves from concept to action. Put Browser (VS Code for the Web) into plain language. The Codespace opens directly in your browser as a fully functional VS Code interface.

speaker: ALEX
text: The room should hear these as checkpoints. Works in Chrome, Edge, Firefox, and Safari. Screen reader support varies slightly by browser - Chrome and Edge have the most consistent behavior with NVDA and JAWS. No installation needed. Some VS Code extensions are not available in the browser version.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Now it sounds like a workflow instead of a wall of instructions.

speaker: ALEX
text: That is where confidence comes from: not from never getting lost, but from knowing how to recover.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Turn that into a path someone can follow.

speaker: ALEX
text: The teaching point here is not the label; it is the move. If you prefer your local VS Code setup with your extensions and settings. That is the difference between guessing and knowing: When connected via local VS Code, your screen reader interacts with your local VS Code installation - all your familiar settings apply.

speaker: ALEX
text: Think of this as 4 checks: Install the GitHub Codespaces extension in VS Code; Sign in to GitHub in VS Code; Open the Command Palette (Ctrl+Shift+P / Cmd+Shift+P); and Run Codespaces: Connect to Codespace. Pause after each step and listen for the confirmation before moving on.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on Local VS Code Desktop. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: The path is straightforward once it is named. Step one is select an existing Codespace or create a new one. The point is not speed; the point is never losing your place.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on Screen Reader Optimized Mode. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: This part earns its place because when VS Code detects a screen reader, it automatically activates Screen Reader Optimized mode. That gives the learner a foothold: you can verify or toggle it manually. That is the difference between following directions and owning the workflow.

speaker: ALEX
text: That becomes easier when you listen for these cues. The editor reads content line by line instead of character by character. Diff views are rendered as accessible text comparisons. Inline suggestions from Copilot are announced on a keypress rather than automatically.

speaker: ALEX
text: The path is straightforward once it is named. Step one is open the Command Palette (Ctrl+Shift+P). Step two is search for Toggle Screen Reader Accessibility Mode. Step three is press Enter to activate. The point is not speed; the point is never losing your place.

speaker: ALEX
text: A solid navigation habit is to prove where you are before activating controls. Headings, landmarks, and the address bar are not trivia; they are your map.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What would you say to someone who is already bracing for this to be too much?

speaker: ALEX
text: Audio Cues: VS Code in Codespaces supports the same audio cues as the desktop version. The next useful detail is concrete: Enable audio cues via Settings → search for "audio cues" or run Help: List Audio Cues from the Command Palette.

speaker: ALEX
text: Now slow down for the part people usually miss. Here is the learner-facing version. The integrated terminal in a Codespace is a standard terminal.

speaker: ALEX
text: Here is the part that makes the next action easier. Screen readers read terminal output in NVDA and JAWS using their document/browse mode after output arrives. In NVDA: Switch to Browse Mode to read static terminal output, then back to Focus Mode to type. VS Code also has Accessible View (Alt+F2 when cursor is in the terminal) which renders terminal output in a navigable buffer.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Where do you want a learner to place their attention here?

speaker: ALEX
text: Learning Cards: Codespace Accessibility has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: These are the pieces that turn the idea into a usable move. Screen Reader Optimized mode activates automatically -- verify with Command Palette Ctrl+Shift+P then "Toggle Screen Reader Accessibility Mode". Audio cues (error, warning, task completed, Copilot suggestion) work identically in Codespaces and desktop VS Code. In NVDA, switch to Browse Mode to read terminal output, then Focus Mode to type -- same pattern as local VS Code. All VS Code zoom and theme settings apply in the browser-based Codespace -- Ctrl+= increases editor font size. Audio cues for errors and Copilot suggestions supplement visual indicators you might miss at lower zoom levels. Chrome and Edge provide the most consistent rendering of the Codespace UI at high zoom and magnification levels.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That is the kind of detail that keeps a screen reader user oriented.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. The named thing - the heading, tab, field, branch, or button - is the handhold.

speaker: ALEX
text: Here is the moment where the page starts to make sense. Anchor this part on NVDA. No special NVDA profile is required for Codespaces in the browser. This is the part to say slowly: The same settings recommended in Appendix B apply. The durable skill is not memorizing one screen. It is knowing how to find your footing when the screen changes.

speaker: ALEX
text: Listen for the small confirmations in this list. Ensure Browse Mode is on for reading, Focus Mode for typing. Set maximum line length to 10000 in NVDA Settings → Browse Mode. In Chrome or Edge, NVDA's Browse Mode detection is generally reliable.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Give me the version that sounds like an instructor, not a manual.

speaker: ALEX
text: JAWS has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: The useful version is not abstract; it sounds like this. Use Virtual Cursor for reading page content. Switch to PC Cursor (Insert+Z or Num Pad Plus) when inside the editor and writing code. JAWS works best with the Codespace in Chrome or Edge.

speaker: ALEX
text: The next point gives the learner a handle. VoiceOver (macOS) has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: This is where the lesson becomes something you can check. Use Quick Nav (Left+Right arrows) for moving through the VS Code interface. The Rotor (VO+U) surfaces VS Code's landmark regions. If text in the editor is not reading fluently, ensure Screen Reader Optimized mode is active (see above).

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on 6. Keyboard Shortcuts in Codespaces. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: If the interface shifts, 6. Keyboard Shortcuts in Codespaces is still useful because codespaces uses standard VS Code keyboard shortcuts. For someone navigating by keyboard or screen reader, this detail matters: The most important ones for workshop exercises.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on Stopping a Codespace. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: Put Stopping a Codespace into plain language. Codespaces automatically stop after a period of inactivity (default: 30 minutes). The useful version is: Stopped Codespaces retain your files and changes. The interface gets easier when it becomes a set of named places instead of a wall of controls.

speaker: ALEX
text: Walk it in order: Go to github.com/codespaces; Find your Codespace in the list; Activate the … menu next to it; and Choose Stop codespace. The point is not speed; the point is never losing your place.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So the learner is not behind if they stop there and check the page.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. Pausing to verify is not a detour; it is how you keep control of the workflow.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on Deleting a Codespace. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: The teaching point here is not the label; it is the move. Deleting a Codespace removes any uncommitted or unpushed changes permanently. That is the difference between guessing and knowing: Always commit and push your work before deleting.

speaker: ALEX
text: Think of this as 3 checks: Go to github.com/codespaces; Activate the … menu next to the Codespace; and Choose Delete. Each step should leave a trace you can name.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on Resuming a Codespace. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: Resuming a Codespace has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: The path is straightforward once it is named. Step one is go to github.com/codespaces. Step two is find your stopped Codespace. Step three is activate Open in … to resume it in the browser or in VS Code Desktop. If one step does not match what you hear, stop there and re-orient.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on 8. Dotfiles and Persistent Configuration. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: 8. Dotfiles and Persistent Configuration: If you have a repository named dotfiles on your GitHub account, Codespaces will automatically apply it when provisioning new environments. The next useful detail is concrete: To set up dotfiles: create a repository named dotfiles and add an install.sh script.

speaker: ALEX
text: The parts worth keeping in working memory are these. Setting your preferred shell (bash, zsh, fish). Adding shell aliases and functions. Pre-installing command-line tools.

speaker: ALEX
text: Here is the practical turn. Here is the learner-facing version. For workshop exercises that involve running scripts or tests, use Codespaces. Put another way, for quickly browsing or editing a file, GitHub.dev is faster to open. A good GitHub workflow is like a well-run meeting: everyone knows the topic, the next action, and who has the floor.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on Learning Cards: Choosing Your Cloud Editor. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: Learning Cards: Choosing Your Cloud Editor has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: Here is what that changes in practice. GitHub.dev has no terminal -- choose Codespaces when you need to run commands, and github.dev for quick file edits. Both editors support screen reader mode, but Codespaces gives you the full terminal Accessible View (Alt+F2) that github.dev lacks. press the period key on any repo page for github.dev; use the Codespaces dashboard at github.com/codespaces for a full environment. GitHub.dev loads instantly with your synced VS Code theme -- good for quick high-contrast reading of source files. Codespaces takes 30-60 seconds to provision but gives you terminal, builds, and the same zoom/theme settings. Both editors scale with browser zoom (Ctrl+=) in addition to VS Code's own font-size settings.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That is a useful checkpoint before anyone starts pressing keys.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. Checkpoints turn uncertainty into information.

speaker: ALEX
text: Keep the thread going. Anchor this part on The Codespace takes a long time to open. Provisioning a new Codespace can take up to 90 seconds. This is the part to say slowly: If it hangs beyond that, refresh the browser tab and try again.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on Screen reader stops reading after I switch to the terminal. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: The reason this matters is simple: switch to Browse Mode (NVDA: Insert+Space; JAWS: Insert+Z) to read the terminal output, then return to Focus Mode to type your next command.

speaker: ALEX
text: Another way to ground it. Do not treat Audio cues are not working in the browser as decoration. Some browsers mute audio by default for new tabs. That is not trivia. Check your browser's site permissions to ensure audio is allowed for github.dev or the Codespace URL. The workshop is closer to rehearsal than lecture. You hear the move, try the move, and then check what changed.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on Extensions I rely on locally are not available. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: If the interface shifts, Extensions I rely on locally are not available is still useful because some extensions are not compatible with the browser-based VS Code. For someone navigating by keyboard or screen reader, this detail matters: Connect to the Codespace via VS Code Desktop instead (see Section 3) to access your full extension library.

speaker: ALEX
text: This is the part worth saying out loud. Put I accidentally deleted my Codespace before pushing into plain language. If the branch exists on GitHub, you can create a new Codespace from it. The useful version is: Uncommitted changes in a deleted Codespace cannot be recovered.

speaker: JAMIE
text: I like that because it gives people permission to slow down.

speaker: ALEX
text: That is the goal. We want the page to feel explorable, not fragile.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on My changes are not showing in GitHub after committing. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: The teaching point here is not the label; it is the move. You may have committed but not pushed. That is the difference between guessing and knowing: In the Source Control panel, look for the Sync Changes or Push button.

speaker: ALEX
text: A solid Git habit is to know which branch you are on, what changed, and what confirmation you expect before you run the next command.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What should people carry with them after this?

speaker: ALEX
text: Carry the map. Know what page or tool you are in, know which action you are taking, and know what confirmation should follow. If the confirmation is missing, pause. That pause is not wasted time; it is professional judgment.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That is a better way to say it than just follow the steps.

speaker: ALEX
text: Right. Steps matter, but understanding wins. That is episode 31. Next in the series is episode 32, where we keep building the same contributor muscles.
